skill.name: clinvar-database
skill.description: Use NCBI ClinVar references to search variants, interpret clinical significance, compare review status, and plan ClinVar data access workflows.
skill.summary: Guide ClinVar variant lookup, interpretation, and data access workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, and blocker patterns, but review found these are primarily markdown examples for legitimate NCBI ClinVar workflows. The main confirmed concern is that the skill documents shell commands, FTP downloads, and a curl-to-shell installer pattern that users could execute outside the AI environment. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, or malicious code was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Examples for Network Downloads
medium_findings.description: The skill provides shell examples using curl, wget, zcat, awk, bcftools, and command substitution for ClinVar workflows. These are documented examples, not executable skill code, but users could copy them into a shell and fetch remote NCBI resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain real shell commands and remote downloads. The risk is limited because they are markdown instructions for legitimate ClinVar use, not hidden automatic execution.

medium_findings.title: Curl-to-Shell Installer Over FTP
medium_findings.description: The API reference recommends installing Entrez Direct with sh -c and a remote script fetched from an FTP URL. This pattern can execute remote code and FTP does not provide transport integrity.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command directly executes a remote installer fetched over FTP. Even though the source is NCBI documentation context, the execution pattern is inherently risky for users.

medium_findings.title: Bulk Download Script Uses Remote FTP Listings
medium_findings.description: The data format reference includes bash snippets that read ClinVar FTP listings and download selected files. The filename pattern is constrained, but the workflow still depends on remote directory contents and shell execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples include shell execution and remote FTP downloads. The commands target public NCBI data and use a specific filename pattern, so this is a moderate operational risk rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded NCBI URLs Are Expected Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to NCBI ClinVar, E-utilities, ClinGen, and related public documentation. They are necessary references for this research skill and do not indicate data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible public biomedical resources aligned with the skill purpose. No evidence found that these links receive secrets or user data.

low_findings.title: API Key Placeholder Is Not Credential Access
low_findings.description: The API reference mentions adding an NCBI API key placeholder to requests for rate limits. It does not read environment variables, embed a real secret, or send a local credential to an unexpected endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only key reference is the literal placeholder <YOUR_KEY>. No evidence found of environment access or credential harvesting.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Scanner Hits Are Biomedical Terms
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match biomedical acronyms or normal prose, such as PubMed, ACMG/AMP, and methods references. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found biomedical documentation, not cryptographic code. The file set contains markdown references only and no crypto APIs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: A documented Entrez Direct installation command executes a remote script through sh. Users should verify installers and prefer authenticated HTTPS or package-manager installation when available.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is a clear curl-to-shell style execution pattern over FTP. This is risky even when presented as documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: FTP-Based Data Retrieval
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly recommends FTP for ClinVar downloads. FTP is expected for these legacy public data workflows, but it lacks modern transport security.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: FTP usage is explicit and frequent. It is legitimate for ClinVar bulk data examples, but users should treat downloaded data as untrusted and verify sources where possible.

content.value_statement: Genomic variant interpretation requires careful review of ClinVar records, review status, and data formats. This skill provides structured guidance for searching ClinVar, interpreting classifications, and planning reproducible data access.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to search ClinVar by gene, variant, condition, chromosome, and clinical significance.
Summarizes ClinVar clinical significance terms, review status levels, and conflict handling guidance.
Describes E-utilities endpoints for ClinVar search, summaries, record fetches, and related database links.
Compares ClinVar XML, VCF, and tab-delimited formats for different research workflows.
Provides examples for annotating VCF files and filtering ClinVar data with common bioinformatics tools.
Highlights reproducibility practices, including archived releases, access dates, and genome build matching.
limitations: It is guidance documentation and does not include executable integration code or a live ClinVar client.
It is not a substitute for clinical review by a qualified genetics professional.
ClinVar classifications can change, so critical variants need current verification.
Some examples rely on FTP, command-line tools, and large local data files.

use_cases.title: Prioritize variants for review
use_cases.description: Find pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants in a gene and focus on records with stronger review status.

use_cases.title: Annotate sequencing results
use_cases.description: Select the right ClinVar VCF release and add clinical significance fields to a variant call set.

use_cases.title: Explain variant evidence
use_cases.description: Understand ClinVar classifications, star ratings, conflicts, and why uncertain variants need careful interpretation.

prompt_templates.title: Find variants in a gene
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ClinVar database skill to help me search for pathogenic variants in [GENE]. Explain the query terms and what review status means.

prompt_templates.title: Compare data formats
prompt_templates.prompt: I need ClinVar data for [PROJECT]. Compare XML, VCF, and tab-delimited formats, and recommend which format fits my workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Plan VCF annotation
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a ClinVar annotation workflow for a [GRCh37 or GRCh38] VCF. Include data source, key fields, filtering steps, and limitations.

prompt_templates.title: Review conflicting classifications
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ClinVar database skill to evaluate conflicting interpretations for [VARIANT]. Focus on review status, submission evidence, dates, and clinical caveats.

output_examples.input: Find high-confidence pathogenic variants for CFTR.
output_examples.output: A focused search plan using CFTR gene terms, pathogenic clinical significance filters, and expert panel or practice guideline review status. The response explains why lower-star and conflicting records need manual review.

output_examples.input: Which ClinVar file should I use for pipeline annotation?
output_examples.output: A format recommendation that compares VCF for coordinate-based annotation, XML for full submission details, and tab-delimited files for quick summaries. It includes genome build and release date checks.

output_examples.input: Explain what a VUS means in ClinVar.
output_examples.output: A plain-language explanation that uncertain significance means evidence is insufficient for classification. The response warns against using VUS records for direct medical decisions without professional review.

best_practices: Record the ClinVar release, genome build, and access date for every analysis.
Prefer expert panel or practice guideline records when prioritizing clinical relevance.
Treat shell examples and downloaded data as untrusted until commands and sources are reviewed.
anti_patterns: Do not use ClinVar classifications alone for diagnosis or treatment decisions.
Do not mix GRCh37 and GRCh38 coordinates without explicit liftover or validation.
Do not ignore conflicting interpretations, low review status, or outdated submissions.

faq.question: Does this skill query ClinVar directly?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and reference material for using ClinVar web, API, and bulk data resources.

faq.question: Can I use it for clinical diagnosis?
faq.answer: No. ClinVar data must be reviewed in context by qualified genetics professionals before clinical use.

faq.question: Which ClinVar format should I choose?
faq.answer: Use VCF for pipeline annotation, XML for detailed evidence, and tab-delimited files for quick summaries.

faq.question: Why does review status matter?
faq.answer: Review status indicates evidence strength. Expert panel and practice guideline records usually carry more confidence.

faq.question: Are conflicting interpretations supported?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explains how to identify conflicts and review submitter evidence, dates, and criteria.

faq.question: What security precautions should users take?
faq.answer: Review shell commands before running them, prefer secure downloads when available, and verify large external data files.
